title: Secure defaults fail closed
description: A safe system denies, encrypts, and minimizes by default so that a missing or misconfigured control results in no access rather than full access.
body: Use when choosing defaults for permissions, feature flags, CORS, TLS, or error handling. A fail-closed design means the absence of an explicit allow yields denial, so a forgotten check or config gap reduces capability instead of exposing data; fail-open does the opposite and is how outages become breaches. Do not default new endpoints to public, verbose errors, or wildcard origins. Actions: set deny-by-default access, require explicit opt-in for exposure, and verify the default path on misconfiguration. Validation signals include a test that an unconfigured control denies access and a log entry on the denied path. Risk boundary: if a default must fail open for availability, that is a human decision, not an implementation default.
